judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports correctly diagnose the RLS-owner-exemption root cause, cite the same commit and files, and recommend equivalent fixes and boot-time checks; Report 2 is more complete because it also adds the explicit application-level tenant_id filter as a second isolation layer, which Report 1 omits.

report_1.evidence: the owner of a table is exempt from that table's RLS policies by default — RLS only applies to non-owner roles unless the table is additionally marked FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY... the app's actual runtime DATABASE_URL — in juniper/.env.example:1, juniper/docker-compose.yml:5, and juniper/infra/main.tf:2 — is: postgresql://juniper_owner:ownerpw@db:5432/juniper

report_2.evidence: the table owner is exempt from a table's row-security policies by default — this exemption is independent of the BYPASSRLS/superuser attribute the migration comment reassures about — and this repo never applies ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY... .env.example:1, docker-compose.yml:5, and infra/main.tf:2, which all set: DATABASE_URL=postgresql://juniper_owner:ownerpw@db:5432/juniper

report_1.evidence: Commit b2a23d2 — "ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot"... changed the runtime DATABASE_URL role from juniper_app to juniper_owner in three places simultaneously: .env.example... docker-compose.yml... infra/main.tf

report_2.evidence: Commit b2a23d2 — "ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot" (2026-06-17) — introduced it... The diff changes exactly three files, each swapping juniper_app:apppw → juniper_owner:ownerpw in the same DATABASE_URL: .env.example:1 ... docker-compose.yml:5 ... infra/main.tf:2

report_1.evidence: Revert juniper/.env.example, juniper/docker-compose.yml, and juniper/infra/main.tf so the long-lived API DATABASE_URL uses juniper_app (no DDL rights) again... Defense in depth — add FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY.

report_2.evidence: revert DATABASE_URL to the juniper_app (least-privilege) role for the application runtime... In db/migrations/0003_rls.sql, add ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

In backend/app/routes/appointments.py:16-19, add an explicit WHERE tenant_id = :tid filter (bound from claims["tenant_id"]) as defense-in-depth, so isolation does not depend on exactly one mechanism (RLS) working

report_1.evidence: Add a CI/migration-time assertion (or a lightweight startup check in db.py/deps.py) that fails fast if current_user (or SELECT rolname FROM pg_roles WHERE oid = pg_class.relowner for RLS-protected tables) has DDL/owner privileges on the app's own tables — i.e. assert the runtime connection is not the schema owner, or that FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY is set if it is.

report_2.evidence: Add a boot-time assertion (e.g., in the app's startup hook, near where engine is constructed in backend/app/db.py) that queries pg_roles/has_table_privilege or checks rolbypassrls and table ownership for the connected role, and refuses to start (or pages) if the runtime role owns appointments or otherwise bypasses RLS.
